title: Zine cover design basics
subtitle: What makes a self-published cover work at print scale
excerpt: A zine cover needs to do three things on a photocopied page: carry a readable title, signal the contents, and hold up when the tonal range of a printed image collapses to black and white. This article covers the design decisions that govern each of those needs.
sourceNotes: Zine cover design draws on the photocopier aesthetic tradition of DIY publishing from the late-twentieth century, and on general print-design principles. No pre-1928 public-domain source covers this specific form. The design principles described here are synthesised from widely documented practice in the independent publishing community.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding method where the pages are folded inside each other and stapled through the spine fold. The standard binding for short zines of up to about 20 pages.

The loss of fine tonal gradations that occurs when a grey or photographic image is photocopied. The copier converts mid-tones to either solid black or white, which destroys fine photographic detail but can add graphic character.

The visual weight relationship between the zine title and any subtitle, issue number, or tagline. The main title should always be the dominant element on the cover; bigger, bolder, or placed more prominently than any supporting text.

content.text: Zines are self-published in small runs, most often on a photocopier or a home printer. The cover has to work at that level of reproduction. What looks subtle or textured on a screen often reduces to an unreadable grey smudge when photocopied. The design decisions for a zine cover are not the same as those for a book or magazine cover. The print process is different.
The three jobs a zine cover does

A zine cover needs to carry a readable title, signal what the contents are, and hold up in reproduction. These three jobs run in that order. A cover that looks beautiful but has a title that is illegible at arm's length fails the most basic test. A cover with a clear title but no sense of what's inside will not draw readers who would enjoy the zine. A cover that only works on a screen is not a print cover; it is a digital image.
The title and how it sits
The
title hierarchy

is the first decision. The title is the dominant element. Everything else on the cover; image, subtitle, issue number; is secondary to it. Breaking this rule produces covers that take three seconds to identify, which is too long at a table full of zines.

Hand-lettered titles work well for zines because they are reproducible at high contrast: black ink on white paper, drawn with a brush pen or a broad marker, copies cleanly. A hand-lettered title also signals that the zine is made by one person, which is part of the point. Typewritten titles (from an actual typewriter or a typewriter-style font) carry the same information about making. Both approaches survive photocopying.

Placed at the top of the page, the title is immediately visible when the zine is stored in a vertical rack or a box. Centred, the title anchors the composition. Neither position is wrong; both work better than a small title buried in a corner.
Images and the photocopier problem
Photographic images lose tonal range through a photocopier, a phenomenon called
halftone dropout

. The copier's default contrast setting will print mid-grey areas as either solid black or flat white, removing all the subtlety of a photograph. This is not necessarily a problem: many zine makers lean into the effect, choosing images where a high-contrast rendering looks deliberate. A face with strong light-and-shadow, a graphic object against a plain background, a silhouette; all survive the copier well. A delicate pencil drawing with soft shading does not.

Two-tone drawings (linework only, no shading) copy without any loss. A bold linocut in black and white copies exactly as drawn. A collage assembled from cut black-and-white photocopies is already at the right tonal level; copying it again produces no degradation.
Test the image before finalising the cover: photocopy it or print a draft at the intended scale. What you see in the draft is what every copy will look like.
Common design approaches

Large hand-lettered title with a simple graphic element: the title fills the top two-thirds of the cover; a single drawing, photograph, or repeated stamp motif occupies the lower third. Reliable, flexible, works for any subject.

Full-bleed image with the title set over it in white or black: the image covers the whole cover; the title sits in an area of high contrast within the image. This requires a photograph or illustration with a clear area (a pale sky, a dark background) where the title can be read.

Typewriter text as the only element: a typed paragraph or a list fills the page. The title is the first and most prominent line. This approach is clean, lo-fi, and reads clearly at any reproduction level.
How the binding affects the cover
A
saddle-stapled

zine has a cover that is the same weight as the inside pages. The spine fold is exposed; anything near the centre of the page wraps around it. Keep the cover design at least 8 mm away from the centre fold, or the spine crease will cut through the title or a key part of the image.
A pamphlet-stitch zine uses a separate heavier-weight cover (120 gsm or above) which is less likely to tear at the spine but still wraps. The same 8 mm margin from the fold applies.

An accordion zine has no spine; the cover is the first panel, the same size as all other panels. The design has no fold concern, but the cover panel may not be the largest visual element if the accordion is spread out and read in full.
Worked example: three covers for one hypothetical zine
A zine about growing tomatoes at home. Title: TOMATO. Eight pages, saddle-stapled.

Option 1: Large hand-lettered TOMATO in thick letters across the top half of the cover. Below it, a linocut of a single tomato plant; graphic, high contrast, clear in reproduction. Issue number in small type at the foot.

Option 2: A full-page black-and-white photograph of tomatoes on a vine, photocopied at the contrast setting that produces bold dark fruits against a lighter background. TOMATO set in white on a dark area of the image. This works if the photograph has enough contrast; it fails if the tones are flat.

Option 3: TOMATO in typewriter font, large, centred at the top. Below it: 'A small guide to growing from seed, with notes on watering and the day everything finally turned red.' All text. Clear, readable, deliberately plain.

All three communicate different things about the same zine. The first signals handmade and illustrated. The second signals documentary. The third signals writing-led and matter-of-fact. None is wrong; the right choice depends on what the contents deliver.
